source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure That 'cloudsql.enable_pgaudit' Database Flag for each Cloud Sql Postgresql Instance Is Set to 'on' For Centralized Logging

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure That 'cloudsql.enable_pgaudit' Database Flag for each Cloud Sql Postgresql Instance Is Set to 'on' For Centralized Logging
input_row.description: Ensure cloudsql.enable_pgaudit database flag for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance is set to on to allow for centralized logging.
input_row.rationale: As numerous other recommendations in this section consist of turning on flags for logging purposes, your organization will need a way to manage these logs. You may have a solution already in place. If you do not, consider installing and enabling the open source pgaudit extension within PostgreSQL and enabling its corresponding flag of cloudsql.enable_pgaudit. This flag and installing the extension enables database auditing in PostgreSQL through the open-source pgAudit extension. This extension provides detailed session and object logging to comply with government, financial, & ISO standards and provides auditing capabilities to mitigate threats by monitoring security events on the instance. Enabling the flag and settings later in this recommendation will send these logs to Google Logs Explorer so that you can access them in a central location. to This recommendation is applicable only to PostgreSQL database instances. Impact: Enabling the pgAudit extension can lead to increased data storage requirements and to ensure durability of pgAudit log records in the event of unexpected storage issues, it is recommended to enable the Enable automatic storage increases setting on the instance. Enabling flags via the command line will also overwrite all existing flags, so you should apply all needed flags in the CLI command. Also flags may require a restart of the server to be implemented or will break existing functionality so update your servers at a time of low usage.
input_row.audit: Determining if the pgAudit Flag is set to 'on' From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the instance to open its Overview page. 3. Click Edit. 4. Scroll down and expand Flags. 5. Ensure that cloudsql.enable_pgaudit flag is set to on. From Google Cloud CLI Run the command by providing <INSTANCE_NAME>. Ensure the value of the flag is on. gcloud sql instances describe <INSTANCE_NAME> --format="json" | jq '.settings|.|.databaseFlags[]|select(.name=="cloudsql.enable_pgaudit")|.value ' Determine if the pgAudit extension is installed 1. Connect to the the server running PostgreSQL or through a SQL client of your choice. 2. Run the following command SELECT * FROM pg_extension; 3. If pgAudit is in this list. If so, it is installed. Determine if Data Access Audit logs are enabled for your project and have sufficient privileges 1. From the homepage open the hamburger menu in the top left. 2. Scroll down to IAM & Adminand hover over it. 3. In the menu that opens up, select Audit Logs 4. In the middle of the page, in the search box next to filter search for Cloud Composer API 5. Select it, and ensure that both 'Admin Read' and 'Data Read' are checked. Determine if logs are being sent to Logs Explorer 1. From the Google Console home page, open the hamburger menu in the top left. 2. In the menu that pops open, scroll down to Logs Explorer under Operations. 3. In the query box, paste the following and search resource.type="cloudsql_database" logName="projects/<your-project- name>/logs/cloudaudit.googleapis.com%2Fdata_access" protoPayload.request.@type="type.googleapis.com/google.cloud.sql.audit.v1.PgA uditEntry" 4. If it returns any log sources, they are correctly setup.
input_row.remediation: Initialize the pgAudit flag From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the instance to open its Overview page. 3. Click Edit. 4. Scroll down and expand Flags. 5. To set a flag that has not been set on the instance before, click Add item. 6. Enter cloudsql.enable_pgaudit for the flag name and set the flag to on. 7. Click Done. 8. Click Save to update the configuration. 9. Confirm your changes under Flags on the Overview page. From Google Cloud CLI Run the below command by providing <INSTANCE_NAME> to enable cloudsql.enable_pgaudit flag. gcloud sql instances patch <INSTANCE_NAME> --database-flags cloudsql.enable_pgaudit=on Note: RESTART is required to get this configuration in effect. Creating the extension 1. Connect to the the server running PostgreSQL or through a SQL client of your choice. 2. Run the following command as a superuser. CREATE EXTENSION pgaudit; Updating the previously created pgaudit.log flag for your Logging Needs From Console: Note: there are multiple options here. This command will enable logging for all databases on a server. Please see the customizing database audit logging reference for more flag options. 1. Go to https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the instance to open its Overview page. 3. Click Edit. 4. Scroll down and expand Flags. 5. To set a flag that has not been set on the instance before, click Add item. 6. Enter pgaudit.log=all for the flag name and set the flag to on. 7. Click Done. 8. Click Save to update the configuration. 9. Confirm your changes under Flags on the Overview page. From Command Line: Run the command gcloud sql instances patch <INSTANCE_NAME> --database-flags \ cloudsql.enable_pgaudit=on,pgaudit.log=all Determine if logs are being sent to Logs Explorer 1. From the Google Console home page, open the hamburger menu in the top left. 2. In the menu that pops open, scroll down to Logs Explorer under Operations. 3. In the query box, paste the following and search resource.type="cloudsql_database" logName="projects/<your-project- name>/logs/cloudaudit.googleapis.com%2Fdata_access" protoPayload.request.@type="type.googleapis.com/google.cloud.sql.audit.v1.PgA uditEntry" If it returns any log sources, they are correctly setup. Default Value: By default cloudsql.enable_pgaudit database flag is set to off and the extension is not enabled. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags#list-flags-postgres 2. https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/pg-audit#enable-auditing-flag 3. https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/pg-audit#customizing-database-audit- logging 4. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/audit/configure-data-access#config- console-enable Additional Information: WARNING: This patch modifies database flag values, which may require your instance to be restarted. Check the list of supported flags - https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags - to see if your instance will be restarted when this patch is submitted. Note: Configuring the 'cloudsql.enable_pgaudit' database flag requires restarting the Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance.
input_row.impact: Enabling the pgAudit extension can lead to increased data storage requirements and to ensure durability of pgAudit log records in the event of unexpected storage issues, it is recommended to enable the Enable automatic storage increases setting on the instance. Enabling flags via the command line will also overwrite all existing flags, so you should apply all needed flags in the CLI command. Also flags may require a restart of the server to be implemented or will break existing functionality so update your servers at a time of low usage.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags#list-flags-postgres 2. https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/pg-audit#enable-auditing-flag 3. https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/pg-audit#customizing-database-audit- logging 4. https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/audit/configure-data-access#config- console-enable Additional Information: WARNING: This patch modifies database flag values, which may require your instance to be restarted. Check the list of supported flags - https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/postgres/flags - to see if your instance will be restarted when this patch is submitted. Note: Configuring the 'cloudsql.enable_pgaudit' database flag requires restarting the Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instance.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The audit process for control 6.2.8 can be fully automated because it involves checking specific configuration settings for Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances and related logging configurations using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) CLI. These settings are exposed via GCP APIs, and the audit process is explicitly detailed with CLI commands in the provided fields. 

The rationale and audit sections indicate that the settings like the `cloudsql.enable_pgaudit` flag and `pgaudit.log` flag can be verified using the Google Cloud CLI. Additionally, the presence of logs in the Google Logs Explorer can also be checked via a specific query, which indicates that all resources and settings can be programmatically accessed and validated. 

Thus, given the ability to query these properties through APIs and the CLI, this control is best suited for an automated audit approach.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_sql_instance_pgaudit_flag_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command to describe Cloud SQL instances and verify the `cloudsql.enable_pgaudit` flag:
  ```bash
  gcloud sql instances describe <INSTANCE_NAME> --format="json" | jq '.settings|.|.databaseFlags[]|select(.name=="cloudsql.enable_pgaudit")|.value'
  ```
  - Ensure the output equals "on" to pass the audit for that instance.

- Check if the `pgaudit` extension is installed:
  - Connect to the PostgreSQL instance and run:
    ```sql
    SELECT * FROM pg_extension;
    ```
    - Verify that `pgaudit` is listed among the installed extensions.

- Validate that Data Access Audit logs are enabled for the project:
  - Navigate to IAM & Admin > Audit Logs in the console and ensure both 'Admin Read' and 'Data Read' are checked for the Cloud Composer API.

- Confirm logs are being sent to the Google Logs Explorer:
  - Use the specified query in the Logs Explorer query box to validate that there are entries present indicating proper log setup:
    ```
    resource.type="cloudsql_database" logName="projects/<your-project-name>/logs/cloudaudit.googleapis.com%2Fdata_access" protoPayload.request.@type="type.googleapis.com/google.cloud.sql.audit.v1.PgAuditEntry"
    ```
  - A positive result implies that logs are correctly set up, confirming compliance.

By automating these checks, the audit process becomes more efficient and less prone to human error, providing consistent and repeatable compliance verification across all relevant Cloud SQL PostgreSQL instances within a GCP environment.
step2_review:
---

### 1. **APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. All required checks can be performed via GCP APIs or the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) CLI. Specifically:
    - The `cloudsql.enable_pgaudit` flag is visible in the Cloud SQL instance configuration and accessible via `gcloud sql instances describe`.
    - The presence of the `pgaudit` extension can be checked programmatically via SQL queries (using automated scripts with `psql` or Cloud SQL Auth Proxy).
    - Audit log configuration and log presence can be verified via the Cloud Logging API or `gcloud logging` commands.
- **GCP SDK Capabilities:**  
  All checks are supported by GCP APIs and CLI. No step requires manual console inspection if proper service account permissions and connectivity are in place.

---

### 2. **TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 7/10

- **Strengths:**
    - The use of `gcloud sql instances describe` with JSON output and `jq` is correct for checking instance flags.
    - The SQL query for checking installed extensions is valid.
    - The Logs Explorer query is accurate for confirming pgaudit log entries.

- **Issues/Concerns:**
    - **Database Connection:**  
      Checking the installed `pgaudit` extension requires connecting to the database. This is not possible via GCP API/CLI alone; it requires credentials and network access. While this can be automated, it is not as straightforward as the other checks and may require additional scripting and secure credential handling.
    - **Audit Log Configuration:**  
      The step "Navigate to IAM & Admin > Audit Logs..." is a manual console action. This can (and should) be automated via the `gcloud logging` or `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` commands, or by querying the [AuditConfig](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/reference/rest/v1/projects/getIamPolicy) via API.
    - **Program Name:**  
      The program name `gcp_sql_instance_pgaudit_flag_enabled` is descriptive but could be more specific (see below).

- **Minor Issues:**
    - The use of `jq` in the command assumes it is installed and available in the automation environment.
    - The log query step could be automated using the [Logging API](https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/reference/v2/rest/v2/entries/list) or `gcloud logging read` instead of requiring console use.

---

### 3. **IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Automate All Steps:**  
  - Replace the manual IAM & Admin > Audit Logs step with a `gcloud` or API-based check. For example:
    ```bash
    gcloud logging sinks list --format="json"
    ```
    or
    ```bash
    gcloud projects get-iam-policy <PROJECT_ID> --format=json
    ```
    and parse for audit log configs.
- **Database Extension Check:**  
  - Automate the extension check using a script that connects to each instance using the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy and runs the SQL query. Ensure secure handling of credentials.
- **Log Presence Check:**  
  - Use `gcloud logging read` or the Logging API to programmatically verify the presence of `pgaudit` log entries:
    ```bash
    gcloud logging read 'resource.type="cloudsql_database" logName="projects/<PROJECT_ID>/logs/cloudaudit.googleapis.com%2Fdata_access" protoPayload.request.@type="type.googleapis.com/google.cloud.sql.audit.v1.PgAuditEntry"' --limit=1
    ```
- **Program Name:**  
  - Suggest: `gcp_sql_postgresql_instance_pgaudit_flag_and_logging_enabled`  
    This is more precise, indicating both the flag and logging aspects.
- **Documentation:**  
  - Note any required permissions (e.g., roles/cloudsql.admin, roles/logging.viewer) for automation scripts.
- **Error Handling:**  
  - Add logic for handling instances where the flag or extension is missing, or where logs are not found.

---

### 4. **CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is fundamentally correct in approach and feasible with GCP APIs/CLI. Minor improvements are needed for full automation and technical completeness.

---

### 5. **FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** MODIFY

- **Modify** the automation details as follows:
    - Replace any manual console steps with CLI/API-based checks.
    - Clarify that the extension check requires secure, automated database connectivity.
    - Use `gcloud logging read` or Logging API for log presence verification.
    - Update the program name to reflect both the flag and logging checks.
- **Rationale:**  
  This will ensure the audit is fully automated, reproducible, and aligned with GCP security best practices.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section              | Assessment      | Notes                                                                                       |
|----------------------|----------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation  | AGREE           | All checks can be automated via GCP APIs/CLI.                                               |
| Technical Accuracy   | 7/10            | Minor issues: manual steps, extension check, program name specificity.                      |
| Improvements         | See above       | Automate all steps, clarify extension check, improve program name, use CLI/API for all.     |
| Confidence           | HIGH            | Approach is fundamentally sound; minor improvements needed.                                 |
| Final Recommendation | MODIFY           | Update automation details and program name for full compliance and clarity.                 |
